title-group.article-title: Association of Physical Activity and Screen Time With Body Mass Index Among US Adolescents

article-meta.aff: 1Department of Pediatrics, University of California, San Francisco, San Francisco
2Department of Epidemiology, University of Alabama at Birmingham, Birmingham
3Center for Health Sciences, SRI International, Menlo Park, California
4Factor-Inwentash Faculty of Social Work, University of Toronto, Toronto, Ontario, Canada
5Exercise and Physical Activity Resource Center, University of California, San Diego, La Jolla
6Center for Wireless and Population Health Systems, University of California, San Diego, La Jolla
7Herbert Wertheim School of Public Health and Human Longevity Science, University of California, San Diego, La Jolla
8School of Physiology, University of the Witwatersrand, Braamfontein, Johannesburg, South Africa

fn.p: Accepted for Publication: December 22, 2022.
Published: February 9, 2023. doi:10.1001/jamanetworkopen.2022.55466
Open Access: This is an open access article distributed under the terms of the CC-BY-NC-ND License. © 2023 Nagata JM et al. JAMA Network Open.

Corresponding Author: Jason M. Nagata, MD, MSc, Department of Pediatrics, University of California, San Francisco, 550 16th St, 4th Floor, PO Box 0503, San Francisco, CA 94143 (jason.nagata@ucsf.edu).
Author Contributions: Dr Nagata and Ms Smith had full access to all of the data in the study and take responsibility for the integrity of the data and the accuracy of the data analysis.
Concept and design: Nagata, Alsamman, Dooley, Kiss, Ganson, Baker, Pettee Gabriel.
Acquisition, analysis, or interpretation of data: Nagata, Smith, Alsamman, Lee, Dooley, Kiss, Wing, Baker, Pettee Gabriel.
Drafting of the manuscript: Nagata, Smith, Alsamman, Lee.
Critical revision of the manuscript for important intellectual content: Nagata, Alsamman, Lee, Dooley, Kiss, Ganson, Wing, Baker, Pettee Gabriel.
Statistical analysis: Nagata, Smith, Kiss, Ganson.
Obtained funding: Nagata.
Administrative, technical, or material support: Alsamman.
Supervision: Nagata.
Conflict of Interest Disclosures: No disclosures were reported.

Funding/Support: This study was funded by grant CDA34760281 from the American Heart Association (Dr Nagata), grant 2022056 from the Doris Duke Charitable Foundation (Dr Nagata), and grant K08HL159350 from the National Institutes of Health (Dr Nagata).

Role of the Funder/Sponsor: The funding organizations had no role in the design and conduct of the study; collection, management, analysis, and interpretation of the data; preparation, review, or approval of the manuscript; and decision to submit the manuscript for publication.
Data Sharing Statement: See Supplement 2.
Additional Contributions: Jiayue Yu, MSc, of the University of California, San Francisco, provided assistance with the Fitbit data. No financial compensation was provided.

Additional Information: The Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study was supported by grants U01DA041022, U01DA041025, U01DA041028, U01DA041048, U01DA041089, U01DA041093, U01DA041106, U01DA041117, U01DA041120, U01DA041134, U01DA041148, U01DA041156, U01DA041174, U24DA041123, and U24DA041147 from the National Institutes of Health and additional federal partners. A full list of funders and supporters is available at https://abcdstudy.org/federal-partners. A list of participating sites and a complete list of the study investigators can be found at https://abcdstudy.org/principal-investigators.html. ABCD Consortium investigators designed and implemented the study and/or provided data but did not necessarily participate in the analysis or writing of this article.
Corresponding author.

permissions.copyright-statement: Copyright 2023 Nagata JM et al. JAMA Network Open.

license.license-p: This is an open access article distributed under the terms of the CC-BY-NC-ND License.

sec.title: Question
sec.p: What are the associations and interactions of physical activity and screen time categories with body mass index and overweight and obesity among adolescents?

sec.title: Findings
sec.p: In this cross-sectional study of 5797 US adolescents aged 10 to 14 years, a combination of high screen time (hours per day) and low physical activity (step count per day) was associated with overweight and obesity.

sec.title: Meaning
sec.p: These findings suggest that high step count may not offset overweight and obesity risk for adolescents with high screen time and that low screen time may not offset overweight and obesity risk for adolescents with low step count.

sec.title: Importance
sec.p: The Physical Activity Guidelines Advisory Committee Scientific Report identified important research gaps to inform future guidance for adolescents, including limited evidence on the importance of sedentary behaviors (screen time) and their interactions with physical activity for adolescent health outcomes, including overweight and obesity.

sec.title: Objective
sec.p: To identify the independent associations of physical activity and screen time categories, and the interactions between physical activity and screen time categories, with body mass index (BMI) and overweight and obesity in adolescents.

sec.title: Design, Setting, and Participants
sec.p: This cross-sectional study used data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study collected from September 10, 2018, to September 29, 2020. Data were analyzed from July 8 to December 20, 2022. A total of 5797 adolescents aged 10 to 14 years from 21 racially and ethnically diverse study sites across the US were included in the analysis.

sec.title: Exposures
sec.p: Categories of total step count per day (with 1000 to 6000 steps per day indicating low, >6000 to 12 000 steps per day indicating medium, and >12 000 steps per day indicating high), as measured by a wearable digital device (Fitbit), and categories of self-reported screen time hours per day (with 0 to 4 hours per day indicating low, >4 to 8 hours per day indicating medium, and >8 hours per day indicating high).

sec.title: Main Outcomes and Measures
sec.p: Participant BMI was calculated as weight in kilograms divided by height in meters squared and converted into sex- and age-specific percentiles in accordance with the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention growth curves and definitions. Individuals were classified as having overweight or obesity if their BMI was in the 85th percentile or higher for sex and age.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Among 5797 adolescents included in the analytic sample, 50.4% were male, 61.0% were White, 35.0% had overweight or obesity, and the mean (SD) age was 12.0 (0.6) years. Mean (SD) reported screen time use was 6.5 (5.4) hours per day, and mean (SD) overall step count was 9246.6 (3111.3) steps per day. In models including both screen time and step count, medium (risk ratio [RR], 1.24; 95% CI, 1.12-1.37) and high (RR, 1.29; 95% CI, 1.16-1.44) screen time categories were associated with higher overweight or obesity risk compared with the low screen time category. Medium (RR, 1.19; 95% CI, 1.06-1.35) and low (RR, 1.30; 95% CI, 1.11-1.51) step count categories were associated with higher overweight or obesity risk compared with the high step count category. Evidence of effect modification between screen time and step count was observed for BMI percentile. For instance, among adolescents with low screen use, medium step count was associated with a 1.55 higher BMI percentile, and low step count was associated with a 7.48 higher BMI percentile. However, among those with high screen use, step count categories did not significantly change the association with higher BMI percentile (low step count: 8.79 higher BMI percentile; medium step count: 8.76 higher BMI percentile; high step count: 8.26 higher BMI percentile).

sec.title: Conclusions and Relevance
sec.p: In this cross-sectional study, a combination of low screen time and high step count was associated with lower BMI percentile in adolescents. These results suggest that high step count may not offset higher overweight or obesity risk for adolescents with high screen time, and low screen time may not offset higher overweight or obesity risk for adolescents with low step count. These findings addressed several research gaps identified by the Physical Activity Guidelines Advisory Committee Scientific Report and may be used to inform future screen time and physical activity guidance for adolescents.

abstract.p: This cross-sectional study uses data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study to assess the association of physical activity and screen time with overweight or obesity among adolescents in the US.

sec.p: Received 2022 Oct 27; Accepted 2022 Dec 22; Collection date 2023 Feb.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Currently, the Physical Activity Guidelines for Americans published by the US Department of Health and Human Services1 recommends 60 minutes of moderate to vigorous intensity physical activity per day for adolescents; however, in the last 5 years, the proportion of US adolescents meeting these guidelines decreased to lower than 25%,1,2 with subsequent reductions to lower than 10% during the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic.3 This decrease in physical activity has been accompanied by an increase in recreational screen use time, which has nearly tripled in the past few decades4 and also increased substantially during the pandemic.5

Although it is generally acknowledged that greater physical activity and lower screen use are favorable for the prevention of obesity, the Physical Activity Guidelines Advisory Committee Scientific Report6 identified several important research gaps that could inform future guidelines. First, there remains a lack of recommendations on sedentary behaviors, including screen time during adolescence, due to insufficient evidence.6 Second, there is a need to understand dose-response associations between physical activity and health outcomes using objective measures. Third, the scientific report highlighted the need to examine the interactive effects of sedentary behavior and physical activity on health outcomes in adolescents.6 Therefore, it is important to understand how combinations of physical activity and screen time are associated with obesity and cardiovascular health. Some adult studies have found that sedentary behaviors such as screen time may have consequences for body mass index (BMI), independent of physical activity.7,8,9 That is, even if one is physically active, more screen time may be associated with overweight and obesity.

Few studies have examined the interaction of screen time and physical activity and the association with adolescent BMI.10,11 A 2020 Canadian study12 looked at the optimal combination of physical activity and screen time needed to minimize the risk of overweight and obesity in adolescents, concluding that meeting both physical activity and screen time recommendations was most correlated with lower odds of overweight or obesity. However, this study12 used self-report as a measure of physical activity, which is limited by recall errors and response bias. Because step count is a measure of physical activity that can be objectively gathered across a reasonably extended period of time, it may provide a more accurate measure of activity. Furthermore, step counts provide estimates of accumulated daily movement using a measure that is meaningful to a lay audience, which may make step counts more clinically relevant.

In this cross-sectional study, we addressed these research gaps by examining the independent associations of physical activity (as objectively measured by daily step count) and screen time categories, and the interactions between physical activity and screen time categories, with BMI percentile and overweight and obesity. We hypothesized that high screen time and low step count would be associated with higher BMI percentile and overweight and obesity and that there would be significant screen-step interactions whereby higher step count would offset the negative consequences of high screen time for BMI.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: We analyzed cross-sectional data from year 2 (September 10, 2018, to September 29, 2020) of the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study (4.0 release, involving participants aged 10-14 years), in which participants wore a wireless digital step-tracking device (Fitbit; Alphabet, Inc). Data were analyzed from July 8 to December 20, 2022. The ABCD Study is the largest long-term longitudinal study of adolescent brain development and health in the US. From 2016 to 2018 (year 0), 11 875 children were recruited from 21 racially and ethnically diverse sites across the country. Descriptions of the study sample, recruitment, procedures, and measures have been previously described.13 For the current analyses, participants with missing data for step count, screen time, or anthropometric variables were excluded (eMethods in Supplement 1), leaving a total sample of 5797 adolescents. Centralized institutional review board approval was received from the University of California, San Diego. The University of California, San Francisco, approved this secondary data analysis. Study sites obtained approval from their local institutional review boards. Caregivers provided written informed consent, and each adolescent provided written assent. This study followed the Strengthening the Reporting of Observational Studies in Epidemiology (STROBE) reporting guideline for cross-sectional studies.

sec.title: Screen Time
sec.p: Screen time data were collected using the ABCD Study Screen Time Questionnaire, which was completed by each adolescent.14 The questionnaire was divided by weekdays and weekends, asking participants to self-report how many hours per day were spent engaging with several different types of media, including television shows and movies, videos, video games (single player and multiplayer), texting, social media, and video chat. The time across all types of media was summed to form a composite amount of time spent using screens per weekday and weekend. The total mean of recreational screen use was calculated as the weighted sum (weekday mean multiplied by 5 plus weekend mean multiplied by 2, with the sum then divided by 7). Self-reported screen use has been found to have a significant moderate positive correlation (r = 0.49; P < .001) with an objective passively sensed smartphone app in the ABCD Study.15 Another study16 found that self-reported measures of television viewing were significantly moderately correlated (Spearman P = .54; P < .001) with an objective electronic television monitor and had a high level of agreement, with 95% of values within 4 hours of the mean. Similar self-reported television-watching measures have had acceptable test-retest reliability (7-day test-retest intraclass correlations of 0.76-0.81).17,18 We categorized total recreational screen time in 4-hour increments given that previous studies have identified 4 hours per day to be a threshold associated with overweight and poor mental health outcomes in adolescent populations,19,20,21,22 and other nationally representative surveys of adolescent screen time have used similar categories (ie, 4 hours per day and 8 hours per day).23 Screen time was grouped into 3 categories: 0 hours to 4 hours per day (low; reference category), more than 4 hours to 8 hours per day (medium), and more than 8 hours per day (high).

sec.title: Steps per Day
sec.p: Step counts per day were collected via wearable digital devices (Fitbit Charge series) over a 3-week period (21 days). Previous research24,25 has found that these digital devices offer an accurate and reliable estimation of daily step count as a measure of accumulated physical activity in adolescents over time. We followed best practices to extract, filter, and process data established by the ABCD Study.24,25 Within each adolescent's 3-week study protocol, comprising data collected between November 2018 and November 2020 (year 2), we included all days with more than 599 daily minutes of waking wear time and a minimum of 1000 steps per day, consistent with previous studies.26,27,28,29 Given that previous studies1,30 have identified 12 000 steps as a threshold for meeting the daily physical activity guideline of 60 minutes of moderate to vigorous intensity activity and that 6000 steps per day is approximately one-half of the physical activity guideline dose, total steps per day were grouped into 3 categories: 1000 steps to 6000 steps per day (low), more than 6000 steps to 12 000 steps per day (medium), and more than 12 000 steps per day (high; reference category).

sec.title: Outcome Variables
sec.p: Weight (measured using the Healthometer 844KL High Capacity Digital Bathroom Scale; Health o meter Professional Scales) and height (measured using a carpenter’s square steel tape measure) were assessed by the interviewer at each site. BMI was computed using the standard formula (weight in kilograms divided by height in meters squared2). BMI was converted into sex- and age-specific percentiles in accordance with Centers for Disease Control and Prevention growth curves and definitions. Individuals were classified as having overweight or obesity if their BMI was in the 85th percentile or higher.31

sec.title: Covariates
sec.p: Sex (female or male), race and ethnicity (American Indian, Asian, Black, Hispanic or Latinx, White, or other [no specific racial and ethnic groups were defined in the ABCD data set; write-ins were allowed on the self-report, but these data were not available]), household income, highest parental educational level, and parental marital status were self-reported by adolescents and/or their parents. These variables have previously been reported to be associated with physical activity32 and screen time33 as well as BMI.34 Given that the COVID-19 pandemic occurred during the data collection period (November 2018 to November 2020), we created a COVID-19 variable based on the dates of device data collection (before pandemic, before and during pandemic, and during pandemic), defining March 13, 2020, as the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic in the US.

sec.title: Statistical Analysis
sec.p: Data analysis was performed using Stata software, version 15.1 (StataCorp LLC). Descriptive statistics, including means, SDs, and percentages, were calculated. Linear regression analyses were conducted to estimate associations between exposure variables (recreational screen time and/or step count categories) and BMI percentile (continuous outcome).35 Modified Poisson regression models transformed to risk ratios (RRs) were conducted to estimate associations between exposure variables (screen time and/or step count categories) and overweight or obesity (binary outcome).35 Overweight or obesity was relatively common (>10% of participants); therefore, modified Poisson regression models (ie, using robust SEs) were used instead of logistic regression models because odds ratios produced by logistic regression models are likely to be overestimates of the RRs in such instances.36

For each outcome, we ran 3 models with different exposure combinations: model 1, including screen time only; model 2, including step count only; and model 3, including screen time and step count (joint associations). All 3 models were adjusted for age, sex, race and ethnicity, household income, parental educational level, parental marital status, and data collection period. We examined exposure variables as continuous and categorical variables. We assessed interactions between screen time and step count categories for the association with BMI percentile. Given evidence of significant interactions, we created a 9-category exposure (combinations of 3 screen time categories and 3 step count categories) to estimate the association of each screen-step category combination with BMI percentile.

We conducted a sensitivity analysis using only data collected before the COVID-19 pandemic. All analyses incorporated sample weighting to approximate the demographic distribution of the American Community Survey of the US Census.35 Because each participant was assigned a different propensity weight, only weighted percentages (vs absolute numbers and percentages) of participants were reported. Two-sided P < .05 was considered statistically significant.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Among 5797 adolescents included in this analysis, 50.4% were male, 49.6% were female, 37.7% were from racial and/or ethnic minority groups (3.1% American Indian, 5.3% Asian, 12.0% Black or African American, and 17.3% Hispanic or Latinx), and 61.0% were White, with a mean (SD) age of 12.0 (0.6) years. Adolescents reported a mean (SD) screen time of 6.5 (5.4) hours per day, an overall mean (SD) step count of 9246.6 (3111.3) steps per day over the course of 21 days, and 35.0% were classified as having overweight or obesity (Table 1).

caption.title: Baseline Sociodemographic Characteristics of ABCD Study Participants Included in the Analytic Sample

tr.td: Total recreational screen time, mean (SD), h/d

fn.p: Abbreviations: ABCD, Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development; BMI, body mass index (calculated as weight in kilograms divided by height in meters squared).

Propensity weights in the ABCD Study were applied based on the American Community Survey from the US Census. Because each participant was assigned a different propensity weight, only weighted percentages (vs absolute numbers and percentages) of participants are reported.
This subcategory was listed as other in the ABCD data set, but no specific racial and ethnic groups were defined. Write-ins were allowed on the self-report; however, these data were not available.
Step counts were measured using a Fitbit device.

sec.p: In regression models examining screen time only (model 1 in Table 2), medium and high screen time categories were associated with higher BMI percentile (medium: β = 4.21; 95% CI, 2.18-6.23; high: β = 6.97; 95% CI, 4.73-9.28) and higher risk of overweight or obesity (medium: RR, 1.25; 95% CI, 1.14-1.39; high: RR, 1.32; 95% CI, 1.19-1.47) compared with the low screen time category. In regression models examining step count categories only (model 2 in Table 2), the low step count category was associated with higher BMI percentile (β = 5.98; 95% CI, 2.79-9.16) compared with the high step count category, and the low and medium step count categories were associated with higher risk of overweight or obesity (low: RR, 1.35; 95% CI, 1.16-1.57; medium: RR, 1.21; 95% CI, 1.08-1.37) compared with the high step count category. In regression models including both screen and step count categories (model 3 in Table 2), findings were similar to the models including only screen time or only step count categories. Medium (RR, 1.24; 95% CI, 1.12-1.37) and high (RR, 1.29; 95% CI, 1.16-1.44) screen time categories were associated with higher overweight or obesity risk compared with the low screen time category. Low (RR, 1.30; 95% CI, 1.11-1.51) and medium (RR, 1.19; 95% CI, 1.06-1.35) step count categories were associated with higher overweight or obesity risk compared with the high step count category. Findings when examining screen time, step count, and BMI percentile as continuous variables were similar. A sensitivity analysis limited to pre–COVID-19 pandemic data had similar findings (eTable in Supplement 1).

caption.title: Associations Between Screen Time and Step Count Categories and Body Mass Index Outcomes in the ABCD Study

fn.p: Abbreviations: ABCD, Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development; BMI, body mass index (calculated as weight in kilograms divided by height in meters squared); NA, not applicable; RR, risk ratio.

All models (1-3) were adjusted for age, sex, race and ethnicity, household income, parental educational level, parental marital status, and data collection period (ie, before the COVID-19 pandemic, before and during the COVID-19 pandemic, or during the COVID-19 pandemic).
Model 1 includes screen time but not physical activity (step count) as the independent variable.
Model 2 includes physical activity (step count) but not screen time as the independent variable.
Model 3 includes screen time and physical activity (step count) as the joint independent variable.
Step counts were measured using a Fitbit device.

sec.p: We found significant interactions between screen time and step count categories for the association with BMI percentile. The associations of 9 screen-step categories with BMI percentile (with the low screen time and high step count category as the reference group) are shown in the Figure. Among participants with low and medium screen use, lower step count categories were associated with higher BMI percentile. For instance, among those with low screen use, low step count was associated with a 7.48 higher BMI percentile, and medium step count was associated with a 1.55 higher BMI percentile. However, among those with high screen use, step count categories did not significantly change the association with higher BMI percentile (low step count: 8.79 higher BMI percentile; medium step count: 8.76 higher BMI percentile; high step count: 8.26 higher BMI percentile). Similarly, among participants in low step count categories, screen time categories did not significantly change the association with higher BMI percentile (low screen time: 7.48 higher BMI percentile; medium screen time: 10.30 higher BMI percentile; high screen time: 8.79 higher BMI percentile).

caption.title: Associations Between Screen Time and Step Count Category Combinations and Body Mass Index (BMI) Percentile
caption.p: Results correspond to coefficients from a linear regression model with 9 combinations of screen time and step count categories as the independent variable and BMI (calculated as weight in kilograms divided by height in meters squared) percentile as the dependent variable, adjusted for age, sex, race and ethnicity, household income, parental educational level, parental marital status, and data collection period (ie, before the COVID-19 pandemic, before and during the COVID-19 pandemic, or during the COVID-19 pandemic).

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: In this cross-sectional study of a national demographically diverse sample of adolescents aged 10 to 14 years who participated in the ABCD Study in the US, several research gaps identified in the 2018 Physical Activity Guidelines Advisory Committee Scientific Report6 have been addressed. We found that high recreational screen time was associated with higher BMI percentile and overweight or obesity, even when accounting for step count as a measure of physical activity. Second, we found a dose-response association between step count categories based on data from a wearable digital device (Fitbit) and the risk of overweight or obesity. Third, we found effect modification between screen time and physical activity for screen time of less than 8 hours per day and step count of more than 6000 steps per day. Conversely, higher physical activity did not attenuate the risk of high BMI among those with high recreational screen time; similarly, lower screen time did not attenuate the risk of high BMI among those with low physical activity. Early adolescence is an important developmental period because obesity in adolescence persists into adulthood.37

We found that medium or high levels of screen time (compared with low levels) were associated with higher BMI percentile and overweight or obesity. Findings were similar even when accounting for physical activity in the models. A dose-response association was observed, in which high screen time was associated with the highest risk of overweight or obesity, followed by medium and low screen time. This finding is consistent with results from a previous study,38 which reported positive associations between screen time and obesity in both children and adolescents. Recreational screen time is mostly a sedentary behavior, which can lead to an increase in caloric consumption through mechanisms such as snacking in the absence of hunger and exposure to advertisements that promote unhealthy foods.39,40,41 Our study addresses previous limitations in the literature by including several contemporary modalities in the screen time measure, finding a dose-response association between categories of screen time use and BMI percentile, adjusting for step counts, and focusing on early adolescence.

We also found that medium or low levels of step count (compared with high levels of step count) were associated with higher BMI percentile and risk of overweight or obesity, even when accounting for screen time. A dose-response association was observed, in which the low step count category was associated with the highest risk of overweight or obesity, followed by the medium and high step count categories. These findings build on previous literature42 examining associations between self-reported physical activity and overweight or obesity in adolescents by using an objective and clinically relevant step count. We also used objective step measures from wearable digital devices and categories approximately corresponding to physical activity recommendations, which could inform future guidance for step counts for adolescents.

When step counts were low or screen use was high, there was no significant interaction between screen time and step count. That is, no amount of steps per day attenuated the association between high recreational screen use and BMI percentile. Similarly, the association between low step count and high BMI was not attenuated by minimal screen use. Overall, our findings are consistent with those of a previous study43 analyzing the interaction between sedentary behavior and physical activity and its association with BMI in US adults, which found a significant difference in BMI only by time spent engaging in physical activity for adults watching 189 minutes per day or less of television and/or movies. In contrast, adults watching 60 minutes per day or less of television and/or movies had lower mean BMI independent of their amount of physical activity. Taken together, our results are consistent with those reported in previous studies involving adults43 and adolescents,12 which suggest an upper limit of screen time hours is needed to observe a compensatory consequence of physical activity for BMI. Our study also adds to the literature by expanding recreational screen time to include contemporary modalities (texting, social media, and video chat), which is why the threshold may be slightly higher than the 189 minutes observed for television and movies, and by using step count as an objective measure of physical activity in adolescents. We suggest that step count and screen time categories for adolescents may be used to inform public health guidelines for adolescents.

sec.title: Strengths and Limitations
sec.p: This study has several strengths. First, the study includes a large and sociodemographically diverse population-based sample. Second, for physical activity measures, the study uses objective data that were collected over 21 days (longer than the typical 7-day protocol), which limits self-reporting bias and decreases SE. Third, for the screen time measures, the study includes several different types of screen mediums used by adolescents rather than just computer and television.

This study also has limitations, including its cross-sectional design. The device used to measure step count (Fitbit) is a wearable commercial product but has been reported to have good performance in this age group relative to gold-standard activity monitors.25 Device data were collected for a 3-week period (21 days), which may not be representative of a participant’s physical activity or the intensity of activity over the course of 1 year. To overcome this limitation and explore potential seasonal disparities, future studies may use longitudinal data from digital step-tracking devices over longer durations. While we included only days with more than 599 minutes of waking wear in the analysis, we did not control for variations in wear time that were higher than this threshold, thus producing a potential limitation with regard to differences in participants’ total wear time and variations in wake vs sleep time. Screen time measures were based on self-reported data, which are subject to recall and reporting bias, have low precision, and do not assess content exposure. Even though self-reported measures may be approximations of actual use, it is likely that they are underreported, which could bias findings toward the null hypothesis.15

Future studies, including those using the ABCD data set, could take advantage of more intensive approaches that monitor device use in relation to BMI.44 BMI may be a poor proxy for obesity given that it cannot differentiate fat from lean mass.45 Excluded participants were more likely to be racial and/or ethnic minority adolescents from lower socioeconomic backgrounds; this group has been previously found to report greater screen time and less physical activity and to have greater BMI percentiles.3,5,33,46 This potential selection bias could bias findings toward the null hypothesis. Another limitation is the potential for unmeasured confounders, although we controlled for site and sociodemographic factors as well as the COVID-19 pandemic.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: This cross-sectional study addresses research gaps identified by the Physical Activity Guidelines Advisory Committee Scientific Report by providing evidence that specific categories of screen time and step counts were associated with overweight or obesity. In this study, screen time of more than 4 hours per day and physical activity of less than 12 000 steps per day were associated with a higher risk of overweight or obesity among a racially diverse population-based sample of US adolescents. The results suggest that the combination of less than 4 hours per day of screen time and greater than 12 000 steps per day of physical activity is beneficial to minimize the risk of higher BMI percentile. Future research should use a longitudinal study design and analyze additional chronic disease risk factors and outcomes, including sleep, which would further inform physical activity and screen time guidelines for adolescents.

caption.p: eTable. Associations Between Screen Time and Fitbit Step Categories and Body Mass Index (BMI) Outcomes in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, Excluding Data Collected During the COVID-19 Pandemic
